item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Always sends shivers down my spine
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have two big problems with this. The first is a matter of personal taste; I just don't find the anomaly interesting. Despite O5-11's insistence to the contrary, I don't see this as meaningfully distinct from the concept of Hell, and I've simply heard about plenty of versions of Hell already. It's not an interesting or creative idea of Hell either. Everything hurts really badly forever, the end. Sure, it's horrifying, but not in a compelling way, which is a letdown considering how much buildup it gets.

The second issue I have with this article is something I'm surprised no one seems to have brought up yet, though I didn't go through all the comments, so maybe someone did. I cannot remotely believe that the Overseers would ever allow one of their own to leave for vacation without notice and with no way to contact them. I can handle an Overseer speaking in purple prose, some people really do that. I can accept the idea of the Overseers using anomalies on themselves, it's maybe not the wisest choice, but it's a choice I can picture some version of the Council making. But letting each other vanish without notice? That wouldn't fly at a regular job, much less for leaders of a worldwide top secret agency tasked with the fate of humanity. I know they said they banned the practice after 11 went missing, but I simply can't suspend my disbelief nearly enough to picture them ever allowing it in the first place. How are they supposed to tell if Roger is on one of his weekend trips, or if he's been captured by the Insurgency or something? The logical dissonance was too much for me to stay invested in the article. I did love 11 jumping in with 106 though, that was a great 'oh shit' moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Silly O5s! All that panic and at no point did anyone think to check, with the Foundation's usual clinical thinking, whether that was *a property of the Galapagos islands specifically…*

I mean, it's not like they are heavily populated - if they thought it could be endemic to death period and never been discovered, it'd be even easier for it to be a localised phenominon created by some nasty hiterto anomaly located in the region; which, without anyone going "hang on…" is going uncontained.

Heck, the very response of the O5s could *itself* be due to some sort of memetic effect…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorite SCP's of all time, the concept itself makes me scared and intrigued. One of the reasons I applied for membership was that I could give this masterpiece a +1. So here's a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely upvoted by me too. And, far as I can tell, you're right - O5-1 probably ordered both of them killed as a breach ("oscar" I'm pretty sure meaning "rogue O5")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bring us back. Get us out.

That is all I needed from this article to get the +1. The desperate tone is sold very well by the slow, maddened acceptance of defeat throughout the article.

I will say I am always confused as to why she's killed. Did O5-1 order the MTU to kill both O5 that left the room?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope this doesn't count as a necropost, but in my interpretation that's a part of the anomaly. It seems to make you deathly afraid and might have some effects on your logical thought, at least the moment you're inflicted with it - O5-11 could've learnt to think radically after months of quarantine, but still be affected by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My headcannon is that this is the answer as to 'why?'

http://www.scpwiki.com/scp-5000

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In all honesty speaking, my biggest gripe with this SCP isn't the SCP itself- but rather the extraordinarily childlike response of the O5 Council. Rather than question whether Roger was an outlier of sorts (Because let's be frank here- one O5 with plenty of interactions with the anomalous shouldn't, under any means, count as 'normal') and investigate further- they just went right ahead screeching like a bunch of banshees about 'death', rather then even bother to investigate further.

It's the equivalent of judging an entire basket of eggs based on the one that got shat out by a radioactive goose who mated with a bulldog- you really can't judge the entire basket based on that. -1.

item.title: sorry, can't help myself
item.content:encoded:
minor thing that threw me off: I don't think the term "diaspora" is correct here.

+ Definition
- Hide whatever

Definition
Noun
diaspora (plural diasporas)
1. The dispersion of the Jews among the Gentiles after the Captivity.
2. Any similar dispersion.

  • The African diaspora caused a melding of cultures, both African cultures and Western ones, in many places.

3. A group so dispersed, especially Jews outside of the land of Israel.
4. The regions where such a dispersed group (especially the Jews) resides, taken collectively.

  • Jews in the diaspora often have a different perspective of anti-Semitism from Israeli Jews.

5. Any dispersion of an originally homogeneous entity, such as a language or culture.

  • 1988, Joseph Foley, New Englishes: the case of Singapore, page 1:

Small wonder that there should have been in recent years fresh talk of the diaspora of English into several mutually incomprehensible languages


Based on this, I'd say you could make a case for the fifth definition since the deceased could be referred to as "an originally homogenous entity" and I assume that is what the author intended - to describe a sense of dispersion. It wouldn't be a very convincing case, though. I understand it's supposed to add to the purple prose, which I otherwise think is quite well done. This, however, breaks immersion quite significantly.

Similar issue with the term "shibboleth".

+ Definition
- Hide whatever

A shibboleth (/ˈʃɪbəlɛθ, -ɪθ/)[1][2] is any custom or tradition, usually a choice of phrasing or even a single word, that distinguishes one group of people from another.[3][4][5]

It's such an uncommon word that people are bound to wonder and look it up on their own. Again, I'm aware this is an attempt to improve the overall tone of the article. I also understand that it is meant to underline the fact that Sheldon's knowledge is exclusive and that circumstance technically disstinguishes one group from another in this case. However, since one group consists of literally only Sheldon himself vs. the other group consisting of everyone else, this just feels awkward and imprecise, if anything.

I suggest changing "his diaspora" to something like "whatever biological remains we were able to retrieve" or something more eloquent.
For shibboleth, I suggest a rewrite along the lines of "…extracting the unique/singular knowledge/info/intel from his brain."

*note that I am not a native English speaker and fully aware that I am on the proverbial thin ice in such matters. To quote Futurama: "I am not very good with words, which is why I find myself in this delicate conundrum."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most awful, terrible, evil thing I could imagine forever. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this article a lot. it is probably currently in my top ten favorites.

I have a question for the author: I noticed similarities between this SCP and the great horror short story "The Testament of Magdalen Blair" by Aleister Crowley. Was that story an inspiration for you, or was it just a coincidence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The point of those would be explaining both why the article was not redacted, and why the bit at the end after they are shot is still there.

O5-7 is, by their own admission, a master of dictation, so it's not surprising that she didn't make many mistakes in her retelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’ve come up with my own personal theory for why this exists. My theory is that at one point in human history an anomalous gene developed that was the source of human consciousness. With consciousness and self awareness, we were far more likely to survive. However, another result of this anomalous gene was this eternally painful death. However, because when you died the gene had already been passed on, provided you had children. So now all of humanity has this anomalous gene that causes this extremely painful existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually really like the concept of this SCP. The writing isn’t perfect, but the concept is extremely frightening.

+1

item.title: Technical idea
item.content:encoded: Integrate into conprocs as sorting algorithm: an “intentionally unlucky bogosort”. It uses a recursed Bogosort to find the correct hash, then intentionally avoids the correct value. Thoughts?

item.title: Re: Moderator Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: That phrase was supposed to be a compliment. This is one of the few entries that actually made me feel real fear. Heck I even up voted this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ultra-necroing this shit in order to drop my lukewarm take that probably no one will read.

For me, death is not the scp, in fact, death has nothing to do with it.
The true horror of this scip, is that in her hysteria, our dear friend 05-7 has unleashed a massively dangerous cognitohazard unto the foundation, and even more than that, has made it impossible to supress, ensuring that the cognitohazard is presented in its full entirety. I believe that the cognitohazard spreads itself by way of that specific description of death. Perhaps the cognitohazard was a) known by 11 before ressurection and "activated" upon his death/comeback, b) known to 11 after first death (which in that case would make it known to all dead individuals, which would make ressurection a huge fucking danger), or c) somehow created by the council during the ressurection process.

In whatever case, my theory is that the cognitohazard existed in the "realm" or "state" of death, and that in bringing 11 back, the 05s unadvertantly unleashed it upon the whole world, and 7, stupidly, gave it a permanent place to stay in the world.

What does it do? No idea, given that all the people who know what it does are either dead, memory wiped, or hanging with 106. And i don't think we'll get to know the long term effects of exposure to the cognitohazard seeing as the containment procedures include a memetic kill agent and a time period in which the in-universe reader has to do the shit he needs to do before getting mind-wiped/killed. However, given the fact that the council went absolutely stupid as soon as they were exposed, and that the containment measures are that no one can even look at this shit, the cognitohazard probably causes some extremely grim shit, and if the containment measures are so extreme, it can probably be assumed that the widespread propagation of the cognitohazard will cause a k-class scenario.

anyways, that's just my take.

item.title: Moderator Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
While you're certainly free to feel however you want about an article, calling someone's written works "unholy blasphemy" is uncalled for.

In the future, please be more circumspect about what language you use to describe other people's work, especially subjective judgements.

item.title: Dafuq
item.content:encoded: I know this format didnt set well with everyone but Christ on his cross dude this idea scared the everliving shite out of. I felt like I had wash my eyes out with bleach and holy water after reading this. As a religious person this terrified me even more. While i might disagree with the atheists their afterlife, or lack there of, is infinitely better than this unholy blashemy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have many problems with this article.

However, since others have mentioned them already, I'll just go with one:

What's the point of saying "no editing the recording whatsoever so don't misspeak" and "I only have 7 minutes left" if O5-7 still manages to very fluently, vividly, and lengthily detail the true nature of death? (Also, what's the actual SCP? Death? The knowledge of death's nature? This is confusing.)

item.title: Re: Referencing with effect
item.content:encoded: The numbers shifting is actually an embedded GIF image. While it could be possible to recolor the image to have it as red, and then make the image link to the article, it would be a real pain to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: same

item.title: Re: How is this anomalous?
item.content:encoded:
Experiencing things when your brain Is dead ain't normal.

We are not out atoms, as we act as a Ship of Theseus. How do you explain the idea that "After you die your perception goes on for the rest of eternity" in scientifical terms?
Exactly, you don't. It's not scientifical. And "normalcy", in the SCPverse, Is what The Foundation declares It to be. As of now "normal" implies some degree of scientifical understanding.

This, on the other hand, implies that this is how Death really is.
As the article is tagged as "Infohazard" I personally prefer the theory that IT IS an anomalous process that gives your soul/whatever the "privilege" to experience this afterlife.
Personally, I prefer such a version because the other possibility (The Foundation ruled that the real afterlife is just too scary and therefore must be hidden) doesn't belong to the main-list, IMO.

I am in doubt between upvoting this or let it at 0. While the concept is extremely cool, I would have prefered it as a tale, but I also see how the format screw and the concept that this fear is so strong that it breaks O5s into tears work better as an article rather than a tale.
I will upvote it later, maybe.

P.S. Also, goddammit OP, do not humanize O5. They are all cool and cold and bastards in my canon (I love Resurrection)

item.title: Referencing with effect
item.content:encoded: Is there a way to get a page link to this while retaining the shifting number effect?

item.title: Re: How is this anomalous?
item.content:encoded:
It is indeed a memetic hazard, clearly an infohazard.

The knowledge of such event made them go crazy. They immediately acted irrationally. This is important as the O5 council, the most important people on the planet, would never behave in such a manner to "[pound a] shoe on the table and [holler] that we must dig a channel from the Astrakhan spring to the Mediterranean Sea."

Also, the file itself is contained through seemingly anomalous means, completely unable to be deleted or modified. The knowledge itself is attempted to be spread and essentially drives people crazy, be it due to their own perception via the knowledge (unlikely a sole reason) or the actual information itself - which wouldn't be very surprising, seeing that they just gained such important information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've never changed my upvote after reading a comment before.

This is a first.

I reread the article afterwards, and began to feel the same disappointment at the rash conclusion reached by the Council immediately after O5-7's testimony. I agree that the idea, the driving emotion behind this SCP, is a powerful one; but that the execution, in this regard, does not match.

Your comment is a lot like a DAMMERUNG class cognitohazard.
Your comment is a lot like a DAMMERUNG class cognitohazard:

I would have been fine it I hadn't read it. But now that I have, I'm resigned to waiting for a popular article to change before deciding to upvote it. A fate worse than death…

item.title: How is this anomalous?
item.content:encoded:
First off, I'd like to say that the idea is sound, and remarkably well written.

However, were this a short story with references to SCP-verse removed, there would be no way in hell I could think this an SCP. Even with the references, I still can't think of this as an SCP.

It's not an entity, not a phenomenon, not a place, not even a memetic hazard.
It's a non-anomalous existential horror revelation, and basically the answer to one of the oldest questions mankind asked itself.

Also, I'm pretty sure there's regularly wackos claiming this kind of stuff (or even worse) IRL, and nothing's come out of it - unless you actually have widespread resurrection, and all the resurrected claiming exactly the same thing, the risks this poses as a cognitohazard are basically nil, since the masses would simply brush it off as way too outlandish and baseless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every time I reread this, I get a little bit more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I read it for the first time, I thought the Description to be a lie and the article to be a sabotage attack, perhaps against O5 council. But authors of "End of Death" hub seem to have another opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is block in Dammerung Eyes Only an actual digital signature or just a bunch of random symbols?

item.title: Less is more
item.content:encoded:
Super cool underlying concept about death in here!

But are we just going to keep killing or mindwiping the entire 05 council in every skip published from here on? They're more disposable than D-class these days. I think we should make an effort to stop killing them all the time, it doesn't really add gravitas. I also think the tech talk at the start is too long - the basic hook isn't really related to it. Shorten that up a bit and it would keep the focus on the underlying concept, which is really really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I may be in the minority here but I love the way it was written. I don't find it to be too purple or verbose just for the sake of it. I was engaged the whole way through, which is definitely more than I can say for other SCPs with higher vote counts.

Thinking about it after the fact, yes there are some contradictions to "SCP canon" but it's also generally accepted that virtually anything the council says could be complete bullshit (from what I can tell), so that's not enough to sour the experience for me either. The beginning didn't strike me as needless technobabble either, and the technobabble nonsense and overuse of [REDACTED] etc prevented me from getting into SCP articles for a long while. The length of it made me nervous, actually. The repeated warnings unsettled me. As far as I'm concerned, it works!

I apparently just have weird standards I guess? But I really did like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: None of those methods are very good after the end of the stellar era much less during lepton decay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While breathtaking longevity is now within our power, it may surprise you to learn that immortality is not; and our best theoreticians have recently posited that it will never be attainable.

One two three four five six seven

And it's just the first thousand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The prose with which the afterlife is described is vividly beautiful, and the core concept is certainly terrifying. However, everything around it strikes me as fundamentally over-the-top and absurd, so I'm afraid I have to downvote.

Honestly, cut like 75% of this article, keep the core, and you could have something lovely on your hands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have literally independently thought of and feared this possible afterlife.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had a random thought just now. Can this SCP be assigned to some masochists or something? A sophisticated AI wouldn't mind what the human afterlife is like, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way I interpreted this, the SCP isn't necessarily death, but the idea death is what 05-11 described it as. The Council collectively lost their minds when they heard what they did, and given that they fear death like they obviously do, perhaps this is because what -11 saw wasn't humanity's fate, but the Council's personal hell. If that was how it was, then their reactions would make more sense, given that this would be their greatest fear imaginable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i'd like to point out that is possible this isnt the answer.
i mean, he died normally, buuuut, he didnt come back, nomrally either
so maybe…its the way they brought him back
they something about rewinding the atoms that made up the person, right?
so what if this method went and gathered up the remains and brought them back to the place they were doing the magic shit at. then, what happened is that his mind was the first thing to turn on i guess? so he could know all of that

which honestly doesnt seem that bad.

and people saying this is a tale, i dont know. it does seem like a spc to me. its not the 001 sun scp, so i'd say this is pretty great for a scp file

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Frankly, the fact that not a single person in this tale generates an alternate hypothesis to the one proposed by O5-7 ruins this one for me.

If you've resurrected a thousand people with a thousand methods, and 999 remember nothing while 1 remembers something, it might be that the method uniquely captures post-death memories, or it might be the resurrection process itself that lasts for a subjective eternity and causes all this agony (in which case nobody else has this experience upon dying, just O5-7), or it might be that O5-7 was affected by some anomaly previously in his life, unrelated to the resurrection ritual, which caused him to continue to experience things after death.

To me, this last explanation is by far the most plausuble. Who knows what sorts of anomalies a typical Overseer encounters over their life? Maybe some vengeful pagan god cursed him to never die, a fairly typical curse as things go, and this is what happens if you suddenly lose necessary brain functions while cursed to never die. Or maybe if you hang out within range of the inertia generator too long it gives your soul enough momentum to continue onward after your body comes to rest. Or something like that.

But the second is almost as plausible. When you're building high-level cognitive matter out of quarks, who the hell knows what the subjective content of the actual process of assembly might be? Maybe from the first nanosecond to the second nanosecond, while the neurons begin to form, feels like a thousand years to the experiencer. Or maybe the brain can't be halted and then restarted later, so the closest living approximation the resurrection process could find was one where it pretended that O5-7 was asleep all those years instead, and so at the end of the process it dumped the memory of having a horribly torturous feverdream for thousands of years into O5-7's brain without O5-7 actually ever having experienced it.

Honestly, if I'd been presented with the same evidence as the O5 council, I don't know if I would have even conceived of the possibility that O5-7's experiences were simply natural and universal, what-happens-to-everyone. So for the others to simply assume it, and not even consider the alternatives, is really jarring, especially considering the strong motivation they would have to find some more pallatable conclusion.

And that sucks, because I really want to like it. I really want to feel the existential horror of this idea, be confronted with the sheer intolerability of it all. But I just can't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an impatient person, sorry, but I gotta downvote.

Don't get me wrong, the format is extemely creative, and the writing is nice. I really applaud you for making this bold approach on a site where everybody is extremely judgy like myself. And certainly, I do not have the confidence nor the wordiness to write something of this length.

But reading the whole thing felt like being forced to watch a Youtube video in which the creator is just desperately trying to stretch out the video to 10 minutes long so he/she can put midroll ads in.

And even worse, there did not seem to be any conclusion by the end. You got my attention with the "don't scroll down" and the neat algorithm stuff, but by the time I read all of that, I just can't think why that had to be done. Those voice-to-text logs seemed like a bunch of nonsense drama to me.

If there is some kind of catch that I'm missing, please tell me, I would love to know so that I could change my vote in favor or back to neutral (which I still haven't figured out how to do LMFAO)

Otherwise, my only suggestion would be to just get to a conclusion faster, or to have a really good conclusion for the long writing. Some of the best SCPs are the shortest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamn, this remind me of scp 2774 (the slow burning sloth), once you know it, you're screwed.

Shouldn't this be a elucid/keter class? More likely elucid because it seems more of a infohazard, I mean isn't threre n scps that's actually god?

Reading this scp make wonder what is writer going throigh…

item.title: Re: The Cognitohazard
item.content:encoded: Necro-reply, but I want it here for posterity that, that was my first thought as well. Rather, that the line swung my vote and made sense of all of the weirdness and OOCness (as much as there can be OOC without True Canon).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The beginning has so much technobabble. It was hard to understand. Also, I am not even sure what that note from the technician (?) was about. So it's a cognitohazard/infohazard that affects individuals to be paranoid about death and what's described is the resulting afterlife one would experience due to the hazard? Personally, that's interesting but the execution could have been done better and avoided the many plot holes. Possibly be even better if it were not with the O5. Also, is it really impossible to remove? They could bring a dude back to life but can't remove a cognitohazard that's locked? Oh, this bothers me:

To put it another way: despite 2.4 million years of hominid speculation, Foundation records contain no reliable first-hand testimony regarding what happens to us after we die.

Honestly, this is very hard to believe. The many, countless, things the Foundation have and will see is certain they will know such a thing. I mean, they have gods in containment. Why doubt? It really conflicts with headcanon also. Really, the majority of what she says is conflicting as hell.

Still, I find this SCP to be not what happens after death, but just the effect of a hazard made by something. An O5 that is in his late twenties suddenly dies? That ain't right. I feel he was killed by something that sent him no where but an existence of misery and wanted him to spread the false knowledge of it. To spread chaos which leads to death. Maybe the state of existence described is a lie made by Death itself and doesn't exist but made the O5 believe it did to birth the hazard.

Anyway, a rework of this concept might be needed for its form at the moment is cryptic and confusing, and has plot holes that many have already pointed out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the buildup and plot here, but it all leads to a boring, predictable monologue. However, I remember being scared the first time I read this, so novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't.

+ Ramblings
- Inner Piece

I like to think I'm a pretty prolific reader and even I had to check the way some of these words were used. But that's not the biggest thing. What caught me off most was this isn't the first skip I've read where Overseers have names, and that is usually an immediate +1 for me. I like the relationships presented between the 05 Council, but something seems missing. I don't feel connected enough to 07 for some reason. Between the way she speaks (like a political news analyst trying to impress their audience with verbose, and usually incorrect, phraseology or bombastic words) and how she's telling the story, I don't really feel "05" from this story. That, and combined with the fact that a part of Emergency Protocol-17 is apparently killing two 05-Command members, kinda makes me rethink a lot of head-canon.

Golden Enemy+ Aneurysm thing. I'm still trying to put that one together, but it probably doesn't even matter.

I'm split. I think I get what this is, it's a cognitohazard that makes you afraid to die if you hear about this way to die. Which in its own (while this is very well written and done) is kind of backwards as I'd think that most of 05, regardless of hearing it directly from their buddy-ol'-pal, would still be able to say, "We've been made familiar with situations similar to this in the past. This data is not enough to sway us in any other direction." Leaving the vote 11-1 (or however many members of 05 are in this iteration).

The description got me worked up, I can't deny that. If this is what death is, then it's going to be a literal eternity of shit. I don't know if it's that I'm able to actually perceive this possibility, or fates much worse than even this death have been spoken about on this site before. 7's verbose and bombastic way of speaking (it's almost braggadocios; I swear I hear an uppity British accent with this. No offense to our British members. Think UPR.) makes me think that, even in her final moments, she's still thinking that she, and all of 05, is better than the rest of us.

Sorry. I can't give a full opinionated vote yet. I'm leaning toward a minus vote honestly,
but I'll keep an eye on it. I think (after a few minutes of being away) that it's simply the fact that I aim to write an article featuring 05 in it (it's a goal of mine) and this article somewhat confused me on what exactly is required, enjoyed or not allowed1 when it comes to writing about them.

Footnotes
1. I forget, there is no canon.

item.title: The Cognitohazard
item.content:encoded:
There's one line in this skip that changes it from downvote to upvote for me.
"Oh! Is belief the key?"

To be this line is what cements it as a Cognitohazard (well seems more like an infohazard really) as opposed to just "What happens after death." Basically, in-universe (and I've given it some thought out of universe too), the idea of the afterlife being whatever you believe it is, is an attractive option. If this skip then overrules your belief and makes what happened to O5-11 happen to you regardless of your belief, simply by exposure to the concept, that's downright terrifying. In a "Now you know, and there's nothing you can do about it." kind of way. I think modulum83's scpdeclassified explanation of this is an excellent example of this idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with a lot of the criticisms: the OOC overseers, the feeling of a tale instead of a skip…

However, this idea of an afterlife is so terrifying, it brings fear I didn't even know existed. To be specific, existential (ha) fear. The OOC overseers compound this: this afterlife terrifies them so bad all sense is thrown out the window.

It still makes no sense, but the theory stated above that the real SCP is a cognitohazard saves it. It just makes sense and nicely patches up the plot holes, while making it scarIER.

Plus I have to give props for containment that ammounts to possible years of IT guys building off the previous' works. That's just cool! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made an account just to comment on this one. It's fine, and I wholly understand what you were going for. Fear of death and inevitability and all that. All those parts are fine. Could be better, could be worse, whatever.

What I really wanted to comment on was what you decided to go with for what happens after death. I understand it had to be sufficiently terrifying to carry the whole entry, but it just seems like you tried way too hard and crossed the line into grimderp.

Here's an example: the Sarlacc from Star Wars. "Victims of the sarlacc were injected with immobilizing neurotoxins that caused constant pain, and, while still conscious, digested for millennia." Yeah, that would suck, but the thoughts of "That would suck" are overwritten by "Really? That's just stupid."

My suspension of disbelief went out the window and never came back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Those sentences are very specifically computing terms.

The easiest way to restate it is:

"Using a flaw in the underlying architecture of the processor Erratum 23 lets us delay completion of the program by causing it to recount the article number with an intentionally bad algorithm (currently random sort) against a known bad stack in memory until the process fails, the long term memory swap completes, and the program restarts"

But this loses some of the expertise of the actual statement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Translator from the Korean branch. Is there anyone who can restate the underlinied words more easily?

As noted above, this article ignores the delete command; it cannot be extricated from the database without extensive collateral corruption of other critical systems. Instead, the containment strategy is to minimize the probability of a user discovering this entry by chance. To this end, an un-haltable mainframe process (ID 9000013) repeatedly switches the ordinal designation of this article with that of another randomly selected entry. Normally, when two article numbers are exchanged for administrative purposes, the two entries disappear from the index momentarily. A kernel exploit of Processor Erratum 23 allows us to delay completion of the subroutine by deeply recursing the article renumbering with an intentionally terrible algorithm (currently Bubblesort Stoogesort Bogosort) against a known corrupt stack in extended memory until the thread aborts catastrophically, the index swap completes, and the process restarts. This artificially inserts a delay on the order of 1017 clock cycles between visibility, when a link to this article becomes momentarily visible and accessible from the article index before the process repeats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For anyone who's interested, modulum83 made a stellar analysis of this article on the SCP declassified subreddit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will point out that the Foundation has objective proof of ghosts and other more esoteric SCP's which seem to indicate the existence of souls and other forms of disembodied consciousness. That would suggest that such a thing would make it worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is deeply unsettling, but it left me with a few questions. If your consciousness was scattered, like the O5 said it would be, wouldn't you be unable to feel the pain when you were dead?
It sure sounds better than having to endure constant agony for all eternity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I strongly suspect that the meme is itself anomalous. Ignoring the half-assed argument that it de-facto must be because it's coming from someone brought back with recall of death — I look at two main factors:

1: The article not-including the recording is 100% prevention of an anomalous cogitohazard. It's also weirdly written technobabble in need of editing, but whatever.

2: "Regardless of the truth of O5-11's experience," she said, "it is plain that we have lost all reason. There is only one possible explanation for this." —- Sure as hell sounds like 05-1 is declaring they've been infected by an SCP.

So my read was that something is definitely wrong here, and the idea is an SCP, whether it's directly connected to O5-11, the way he was brought back, what was brought back instead of him, whatever the frack.

Also: From <abrupt laughter> onwards, yeah I guess it makes sense she just ran out of time so it ends in a weird place, but writers tend to cheat and give characters exactly as much time as they need, and that's just normal — and it matters here because I don't think her final words after that are either necessary or sufficient to finish the article. Tho I do think that laugh before the last little ramble does further the set-up "This is an SCP".

But hell, maybe I'm reading it wrong and it's just supposed to be "Death is fracking awful, let's fix it." In which case literally everything about it is kinda terrible.

Tho when it comes to the O5s keeping themselves from dying, the suggestion is via tech, not anomalous methods. Moreover, I didn't read in there any super-human stuff or anything until 05-11's total break from protocols in seeking to kill death. The O5s come off as just normal people for me here, but again, only if there really is an anomaly at hand. If there isn't, then as I said it is kinda terrible.

But, all that does leave out the primary conceit — they brought him back with memories of death and then just made him O5 again… . .Seriously? No fracking way. BS to all that. I ignore it being nonsense purely because I like the story that it allows tho. It's amazing what you can get away with if it makes room for an interesting idea. So, I admit it's bonkers — but I accept it because I like the toy idea of an SCP related to some kind of cogitohazard obsession with a really bad possibility of what comes after death, and therefore how to avoid it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot of effort put into this one but I have to say I'm not really a fan. The prose is needlessly thick and it kind of drags on.

But I think what bothers me the most is how disjointed it feels. The opening feels like it belongs in an entirely different article than the recording, with a text-screw that has no payoff and talking about a cognitohazard that doesn't actually exist.

That's actually a bigger deal than it sounds, as I ended up kind of skimming through the story because I was more interested in finding out about the actual SCP… only to get to the end and realize there wasn't actually an SCP at all. Instead it's just a story with a completely unrelated preamble that seems to only exist to try to justify calling it an SCP.

This ended up really coloring my first impression of the story and after reading through it I spent more time being annoyed at the heavy handed prose and frustrated at what felt like a duplicitous mislabeling of the article rather than actually thinking about the story itself.

On a second read I decided the story itself was alright, but that's not enough to outweigh the other issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not going to disclose my opinion, other than I thought it was genuinely scary, of this but the end of the scp is the exact same reaction in the comments here as the 05. Everyone just lost their minds arguing about the implications of this and how it works and what should we do about it; for very different reasons obviously but the fact is, the base reactions are IDENTICAL. The only thing I can think of that compares to a phrase that is understandable about how I think it works that well is "successful troll is successful," but in a good and not malicious way. The author poses the supposed real answer that is terrible news to learn about dying, and it hits the very well hidden "nerve" of the instinct and desire to survive, HARD. Anyone who has brushed death or are unlucky enough to wonder about your existence knows this primal fear. Therefore because it's a primal fear, given to the brain far before any of the major brain instincts and feelings for the species, the irrational flight or fight response is accidentally activated and people lose their logic and reasoning.

I probably went too deep kind of explaining why this works even though people think and believe it has problems but the main idea is the best fear inducing thing I've read, but I have no idea how make the delivery any better either. Just flat out describing the death effects doesn't work sadly. So I will just say good job, and I hope that more people get what it really means by my description, and be able to look past the flaws possibly only because the writing format is not easy or even possible to convey the effects and details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "After death there is nothing but eternal suffering". Now you don't have to read all that purple prose, thank me later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what, downvoted until the author fixes the quantum bogosort suggestion, that's just ridiculous.
